title: Ax-Lindemann-Weierstrass Theorem (ALW) for Fuchsian automorphic functions
speaker: Joel Nagloo
abstract: Over the last decades, following works around the Pila-Wilkie counting theorem in the context of o-minimality, there has been a surge in interest around functional transcendence results, in part due to their connection with special points conjectures. A prime example is Pila's modular ALW Theorem and its role in his proof of the André-Oort conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Fuchsian uniformizers and special correspondences
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up genus-zero Fuchsian quotients, their uniformizing automorphic functions j_Gamma, commensurator-induced Gamma-special polynomials, and the relation with arithmeticity and Hecke correspondences.

proof_stages.label: Schwarzian uniformizing equation
proof_stages.summary: The uniformizer j_Gamma is shown to satisfy a third-order Schwarzian differential equation whose symmetries identify solutions, and more generally fibers of the equation, with PSL_2-translates.

proof_stages.label: Andre-Pink statement for Hecke orbits
proof_stages.summary: Hecke orbits, weakly special subvarieties, and the Andre-Pink prediction are defined for tuples, followed by the announced theorem proving the conjecture effectively when the chosen coordinates are transcendental up to one algebraic exception.

proof_stages.label: Differential-algebraic embedding of Hecke orbits
proof_stages.summary: By choosing derivations with prescribed constants and passing to differentially closed fields, ordinary Hecke orbits are embedded into differential Hecke varieties, reducing the intersection problem to algebraic relations among solutions of Schwarzian differential equations.

proof_stages.label: Ax-Lindemann-Weierstrass formulation
proof_stages.summary: For geodesically independent functions v_i on an irreducible variety, the Ax-Lindemann-Weierstrass theorem is formulated as algebraic independence of j_Gamma(v_i) and their first two derivatives over the function field.

proof_stages.label: Strong minimality and geometric triviality
proof_stages.summary: The model-theoretic framework of differentially closed fields, strongly minimal differential equations, the trichotomy theorem, and geometric triviality is introduced to reduce algebraic dependencies among many solutions to dependencies among pairs.

proof_stages.label: Schwarzian strong minimality and special-pair conclusion
proof_stages.summary: The Schwarzian equation for j_Gamma is asserted to be strongly minimal and hence geometrically trivial; further pairwise computations and double-coset/commensurator arguments force any algebraic dependence to come from a Gamma-special polynomial, completing the ALW and Andre-Pink applications.

examples.name: Classical modular curve and modular polynomials
examples.note: The case Gamma = PSL_2(Z), with j_Gamma the classical j-invariant and Gamma-special polynomials the modular polynomials, is used as the familiar model for the general Fuchsian terminology.
examples.engagement_reason: The classical PSL2Z modular curve, j-invariant, and modular polynomials are cited as the standard instance of the general gamma-special-polynomial setup, without computing or applying anything specifically to them.

passes.reason: The modular curve for PSL2(Z) and its modular polynomials are used as the standard illustrative case of gamma-special correspondences, not as a failure case.

examples.name: Weierstrass elliptic equation as algebraic group structure
examples.note: A Weierstrass differential equation is cited to show that solution sets of differential equations may carry rich algebraic-group structure, motivating the model-theoretic classification problem.
examples.engagement_reason: The Weierstrass equation is only cited as an example that carries an algebraic group structure, with no construction of the group law or verification.

passes.reason: A Weierstrass elliptic equation is cited as a typical elliptic curve carrying its usual algebraic group law.

A nonsingular Weierstrass elliptic curve with its usual algebraic group law is an ordinary elliptic-curve structure example.

examples.name: Logarithmic base-extension failure of strong minimality
examples.note: A displayed differential equation becomes lower-order after adjoining log t to the base field, illustrating why strong minimality must be tested over all differential field extensions.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the logarithm base extension to show a displayed differential equation fails strong minimality by differentiating after adjoining log t and obtaining a lower-order relation.

passes.reason: The logarithmic base extension exposes a lower-order relation, so the apparent equation collapses and is used to show strong minimality can fail after changing the base.
The logarithmic base extension exposes a hidden lower-order relation, so the apparent strong minimality collapses and serves as a failure example.
The logarithmic base extension exposes a lower-order relation, so the apparent equation is reducible and refutes naive strong minimality.
The logarithmic base extension makes a higher-order equation collapse to a lower-order relation, showing strong minimality can fail.

Introduced to show strong minimality can fail after adjoining log t, so the apparent higher-order equation collapses over a base extension.
Adjoining log t exposes a lower-order relation, so the apparent higher-order equation collapses and fails strong minimality.
The logarithmic base-extension example is used to show apparent strong minimality can fail because the equation reduces to a lower-order relation after adjoining log t.
The logarithmic base extension is presented to show that apparent strong minimality can fail because the equation collapses to a lower-order relation.
The logarithmic base extension reveals a hidden lower-order equation, so it is used as a failure of strong minimality and the expected order collapses.

examples.engagement_reason: The speaker discusses a Painleve example by indicating that differentiating it once more reveals another relation, using it to demonstrate hidden failure of the strong-minimality condition.

examples.name: Painleve II as a strongly minimal example
examples.note: In response to a request for an example, the speaker gives a Painleve II equation as a genuinely strongly minimal differential equation and later locates Painleve equations in the trichotomy classification.
examples.engagement_reason: Painleve II is given as a strongly minimal example and its equation is stated, but the speaker explicitly asks the audience to believe the fact and does not prove or check it.
